title: PCI-DSS 8.3 — Strong authentication: password-only logins against privileged accounts
exclusions: Does NOT de-privilege accounts; detects successful logins to privileged accounts that used single-factor password authentication.
evidence: Saved search 'pci_8_3_password_only_priv' (every 10m) emits: user, src, dest, auth_method, mfa_used, to compliance_summary.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A test privileged login via password-only completes and appears in the UC within 10m.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A privileged login with MFA_push factor does NOT fire.

app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Okta (Splunkbase)
spl:
search (index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4624) OR (index=idp sourcetype=okta:system_log eventType=user.session.start) earliest=-10m
| lookup privileged_accounts.csv user OUTPUT is_privileged
| where is_privileged="yes"
| eval auth_method=case(isnotnull(LogonType) AND LogonType IN ("2","10"),"interactive",isnotnull(factor),factor,true(),"password")
| eval mfa_used=if(auth_method IN ("mfa_push","mfa_totp","webauthn","smartcard","token:hardware"),1,0)
| where mfa_used=0
| table user src dest auth_method mfa_used
description: Detects a successful login to a privileged account that did not complete a second factor. PCI-DSS 8.3 requires strong authentication for all access to system components.
value: Catches the exact condition that has historically resulted in CDE breaches (privileged password-only sessions). MTTR shrinks from weeks to minutes.
implementation: (1) Maintain privileged_accounts.csv; (2) ensure IDP emits factor evidence; (3) schedule UC every 10m; (4) hit opens a P1 and disables the session; (5) monthly CISO review of patterns. Indexes required: `index=idp`, `index=linux`, `index=windows`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`, `sourcetype=auditd;`, `sourcetype=okta:system_log`, `sourcetype=okta:system_log.`.
visualization: Timechart of non-MFA privileged logins, table of top accounts, single value 'non-MFA priv logins (1h)'. Key fields: `auth_method`, `dest`, `mfa_used`, `src`, `user`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Authentication (CIM data model).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `windows`, `idp` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `privileged_accounts.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('privileged_accounts.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `8.3`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI-DSS 8.3 (Strong authentication) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.96: PCI-DSS 8.3 — Strong authentication: password-only logins against privileged accounts." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742), landing events in `windows`, `idp` under `WinEventLog:Security`, `okta:system_log`. Security channel alone produces 2000-8000 events per domain-joined host per day depending on audit policy (~2-8 GB/host/month). Domain controllers generate 10x more.

Configure the input through Splunk_TA_windows/local/inputs.conf on each Universal Forwarder. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = windows
renderXml = true
current_only = 0
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
checkpointInterval = 5
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `EventCode, Account_Name, Account_Domain, Logon_Type, Source_Network_Address, Workstation_Name, Process_Name, TargetUserName, Subject_Account_Name`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=windows earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain privileged_accounts.csv. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** ensure IDP emits factor evidence. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC every 10m. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** hit opens a P1 and disables the session. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** monthly CISO review of patterns. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "PCI-DSS 8.3 — Strong authentication: password-only logins against privileged accounts," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `privileged_accounts.csv` — the identity register that maps accounts to real users and roles. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`is_privileged`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `user`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `privileged_accounts.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval user = lower(user)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 10 minutes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.96: PCI-DSS 8.3 — Strong authentication: password-only logins ag]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -10m
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.96,reg=PCI-DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `user`, `src`, `dest`, `LogonType`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table user, src, dest, LogonType, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `privileged_accounts.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup privileged_accounts.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.96: PCI-DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Timechart Of Non-Mfa Privileged Logins (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Top Accounts (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Non-Mfa Priv Logins (1H)' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI-DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We detect a successful login to a privileged account that did not complete a second factor. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.
